approaches.name: Brute Force O(m*n*min(m,n)^2)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all possible top-left corners and square sizes, checking if each square contains only '1's. Use this for small matrices or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding largest square of 1s by checking all possible squares.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Validate Square of Given Size and Position
ladders.desc: Check if a specific square region contains only '1's.

All cells are '1', return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Possible Squares
ladders.desc: Systematically generate all square positions and sizes in a matrix.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves generating every possible square that could exist in the matrix. The intuition is that to find the maximum square, we must consider all candidates. A square is defined by its top-left corner (i,j) and its side length k. We need to iterate over all valid combinations where the square stays within matrix boundaries.

Algorithm: 1) For each cell (i,j) in the m×n matrix: a) Determine max possible side: min(m-i, n-j). b) For k from 1 to max_possible: i) Process square with top-left (i,j) and side k.

Time complexity: O(m*n*min(m,n)) for just generating the squares, without validation. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This generates the parameters (i,j,k) that are passed to the validation subroutine in L1.

Common mistakes: 1) Starting k from 0 instead of 1 (side length must be ≥1). 2) Incorrect calculation of max possible side. 3) Not considering that k=1 squares are valid even if cell is '0' (should be filtered by validation).

Real code pattern:
for i in range(m):
    for j in range(n):
        max_side = min(m-i, n-j)
        for k in range(1, max_side+1):
            # Now we have square defined by (i,j,k)
            process_square(i, j, k)

examples.trace:
m=2, n=2.
Cell (0,0): max_side=min(2-0,2-0)=2. k=1: square (0,0,1). k=2: square (0,0,2).
Cell (0,1): max_side=min(2-0,2-1)=1. k=1: square (0,1,1).
Cell (1,0): max_side=min(2-1,2-0)=1. k=1: square (1,0,1).
Cell (1,1): max_side=min(2-1,2-1)=1. k=1: square (1,1,1).
Total 5 squares.

m=1, n=3.
Cell (0,0): max_side=min(1-0,3-0)=1. k=1: square (0,0,1).
Cell (0,1): max_side=min(1-0,3-1)=1. k=1: square (0,1,1).
Cell (0,2): max_side=min(1-0,3-2)=1. k=1: square (0,2,1).
Total 3 squares (only size 1 possible due to m=1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Matrix Traversal and Boundary Checking
ladders.desc: Navigate through matrix cells and determine valid ranges for operations.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves two key skills: iterating through all matrix cells and calculating how far we can go from a given position without exceeding matrix boundaries. The intuition is that any operation on a matrix (like checking a square) must respect the matrix dimensions to avoid index errors.

Algorithm part 1 (traversal): Use nested loops: for i in range(rows): for j in range(cols): process cell (i,j).
Algorithm part 2 (boundary): Given position (i,j), maximum square side possible = min(rows-i, cols-j). This ensures the square stays within [0,rows-1] and [0,cols-1].

Time complexity: O(rows*cols) for traversal. Space: O(1).

Connection to L2: L2 uses this traversal to visit each potential top-left corner and uses boundary calculation to determine how large a square can be from that position.

Common mistakes: 1) Using ≤ instead of < in range boundaries. 2) Confusing row-major vs column-major order. 3) Not handling empty matrices. 4) Incorrect boundary formula.

Real code pattern:
rows, cols = len(matrix), len(matrix[0])
for i in range(rows):
    for j in range(cols):
        # Process cell (i,j)
        max_side_from_here = min(rows-i, cols-j)

examples.trace:
rows=3, cols=3, i=1, j=1.
rows-i = 3-1 = 2
cols-j = 3-1 = 2
min(2,2) = 2
Maximum square side starting at (1,1) is 2 (would extend to row 2, col 2).

rows=2, cols=4, i=0, j=2.
rows-i = 2-0 = 2
cols-j = 4-2 = 2
min(2,2) = 2
Maximum square side starting at (0,2) is 2 (would use rows 0-1, cols 2-3).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Compare Matrix Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from 2D array and compare with target value.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves two parts: accessing a specific cell in a 2D matrix and comparing its value to a target (in this case, checking if it equals '1'). The intuition is that matrices are typically represented as arrays of arrays, where matrix[i][j] gives the value at row i, column j. Comparison is then a simple equality check.

Algorithm: 1) Access: value = matrix[row][col]. 2) Compare: value == '1' returns True/False.

Time complexity: O(1) per access+compare. Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: L3's traversal uses this operation to examine each cell's value when processing.

Common mistakes: 1) Indexing errors (e.g., matrix[i][j] when j out of bounds). 2) Confusing row and column indices. 3) Comparing with integer 1 instead of string '1' if matrix stores strings. 4) Not handling different data types.

Real code pattern:
# Access
cell_value = matrix[i][j]
# Compare
if cell_value == '1':
    # Do something
else:
    # Do something else

examples.trace:
matrix[0][1] accesses row 0, column 1.
Value at that position is "0".
Compare "0" == "1": returns False.

matrix[1][1] accesses row 1, column 1.
Value is "1".
Compare "1" == "1": returns True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Arrays and Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Understand matrix representation and basic iteration patterns.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept covers two essential programming constructs: 2D arrays (matrices) and nested loops for traversing them. A 2D array is essentially an array of arrays, where each inner array represents a row. Nested loops allow us to systematically visit each cell: the outer loop iterates through rows, the inner loop through columns.

Intuition: Think of a matrix as a grid with rows and columns. Nested loops work like reading a book: you go through each line (row) and within each line, read each word (column).

Algorithm: 1) Define matrix: matrix = [[row0_col0, row0_col1, ...], [row1_col0, row1_col1, ...], ...]. 2) Get dimensions: rows = len(matrix), cols = len(matrix[0]). 3) Traverse: for i in range(rows): for j in range(cols): process matrix[i][j].

Time complexity: O(rows*cols). Space: O(1) for traversal.

Connection to L4: L4 builds on this by adding value access and comparison during traversal.

Common mistakes: 1) Assuming all rows have same length. 2) Using wrong loop order (columns outer, rows inner). 3) Off-by-one errors in range. 4) Modifying matrix while iterating.

Real code pattern:
matrix = [["1","0"],["1","1"]]  # 2x2 matrix
rows = len(matrix)
cols = len(matrix[0])
for i in range(rows):
    for j in range(cols):
        print(f"Cell ({i},{j}) = {matrix[i][j]}")

examples.trace:
rows=2, cols=2.
i=0: j=0: matrix[0][0]="A", j=1: matrix[0][1]="B"
i=1: j=0: matrix[1][0]="C", j=1: matrix[1][1]="D"
Cells visited in row-major order.

rows=2, cols=3.
i=0: j=0:1, j=1:2, j=2:3
i=1: j=0:4, j=1:5, j=2:6
All 6 cells visited.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP array where dp[i][j] represents the side length of the largest square ending at (i,j). Compute it based on the minimum of top, left, and top-left neighbors plus one. This efficiently builds up solutions from smaller subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximal Square in Binary Matrix
ladders.desc: Find the area of the largest square containing only 1's in a binary matrix using dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the largest square submatrix of all 1's in a binary matrix. The dynamic programming approach builds a 2D DP array where dp[i][j] represents the side length of the largest square ending at cell (i,j). The key insight is that a square ending at (i,j) can only be extended if the current cell is '1' and the cells above, left, and diagonally above-left also form squares. The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 if matrix[i][j] = '1', else 0. This works because the minimum ensures all three directions can support the same square size. We initialize the first row and column directly from the matrix since squares ending there can only be size 1 if the cell is '1'. As we fill the DP table, we track the maximum side length seen. The final answer is the square of this maximum side length (area). Time complexity is O(m*n) as we visit each cell once, and space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP table. This approach efficiently builds up solutions from smaller subproblems, avoiding recomputation. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases (empty matrix, single row/column), incorrectly initializing the DP table, or misunderstanding that dp[i][j] represents side length not area. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the core DP recurrence as the key subroutine.

examples.trace: Matrix: 2x2. Initialize dp. Row 0: dp[0][0]=0, dp[0][1]=1. Row 1: dp[1][0]=1, dp[1][1]=0 (since matrix[1][1]='0'). Max side length = 1. Area = 1^2 = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence for Square Side Length
ladders.desc: Compute the side length of the largest square ending at a cell using its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the dynamic programming solution. Given a binary matrix cell (i,j) and the DP values for its top (i-1,j), left (i,j-1), and top-left (i-1,j-1) neighbors, we compute dp[i][j] as the side length of the largest square ending at (i,j). The intuition is that a square ending at (i,j) can only be as large as the smallest square among its three adjacent directions, because all three must support the same extension to form a valid larger square. If matrix[i][j] is '0', no square can end here, so dp[i][j] = 0. If it's '1', we take the minimum of dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], and dp[i-1][j-1], then add 1. This addition accounts for the current cell itself. For example, if all three neighbors have value 2, they each support a 2x2 square, so adding the current cell forms a 3x3 square. The step-by-step algorithm: Check if matrix[i][j] is '1'. If yes, set dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1. Else, set dp[i][j] = 0. This runs in O(1) time per cell and uses O(1) extra space beyond input. Connection to L0: This recurrence is applied to every cell (except first row/column) in the full problem to build the DP table. Common mistakes include using max instead of min (which overestimates), forgetting to check matrix[i][j] first, or mishandling indices for the first row/column where neighbors might not exist. The real code pattern is a simple conditional and min operation.

examples.trace: Since matrix[i][j] is '1', compute min(2,2,1) = 1. Add 1: 1+1=2. So dp[i][j]=2, meaning a 2x2 square ends here.
Since matrix[i][j] is '0', dp[i][j] is set to 0 immediately, regardless of neighbor values.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Three Values for Square Extension
ladders.desc: Understand why taking the minimum of three adjacent DP values ensures a valid square.
ladders.explanation: The core logic behind the DP recurrence is that to form a larger square ending at (i,j), we need all three adjacent squares (top, left, top-left) to be at least as large as the proposed extension. The minimum operation guarantees this: if the smallest among them is k, then all three support a square of side k, and adding the current cell creates a square of side k+1. Intuitively, think of building a square layer by layer. The top neighbor dp[i-1][j] tells the largest square ending above, which must cover the top edge of our new square. Similarly, the left neighbor dp[i][j-1] covers the left edge, and the top-left dp[i-1][j-1] covers the diagonal corner, ensuring the entire area is filled. If any is smaller, it creates a 'gap' that prevents a larger square. For example, if dp[i-1][j]=2, dp[i][j-1]=2, but dp[i-1][j-1]=1, the diagonal only supports a 1x1 square, so the maximum we can form is 2x2 (min=1, plus 1). This logic ensures we never overcount. The step-by-step insight: Visualize a grid; to have a 3x3 square ending at (i,j), we need 2x2 squares ending at (i-1,j), (i,j-1), and (i-1,j-1). The minimum checks this condition. Time and space are O(1) for this logic. Connection to L1: This is the reasoning behind the recurrence formula. Common mistakes include thinking the maximum should be used (which would allow gaps) or not seeing why all three directions are necessary (e.g., skipping top-left might miss diagonal constraints). Real code pattern is simply min(a,b,c).

examples.trace: All three values are 3, meaning each supports a 3x3 square. The minimum is 3, indicating we can extend to a 4x4 square by adding the current cell (in L1, this becomes 4 after adding 1).
Values are 2,1,2. The minimum is 1, so the limiting factor is the left neighbor only supporting a 1x1 square. Thus, the largest possible extension is to a 2x2 square (1+1).

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D Array Traversal and Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Iterate through a 2D grid while safely accessing adjacent cells (top, left, diagonal).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a 2D array (matrix) in row-major order and accessing specific neighbors for each cell: the cell directly above (top), to the left (left), and diagonally above-left (top-left). The intuition is that in dynamic programming, we often build solutions based on previously computed values, so we need to visit cells in an order where neighbors are already processed. For a DP table dp, we start from the first row and column, then move row by row, left to right. For a cell (i,j), its top neighbor is at (i-1,j), left at (i,j-1), and top-left at (i-1,j-1). We must handle edge cases: for the first row (i=0), there is no top or top-left neighbor; for the first column (j=0), there is no left or top-left neighbor. In such cases, we might initialize these cells separately (e.g., based directly on the matrix value). The step-by-step algorithm: Use nested loops for i from 0 to m-1 and j from 0 to n-1. If i>0 and j>0, access dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]. Else, handle initialization. Time complexity is O(m*n) for traversal, and space is O(1) for traversal itself (excluding storage). Connection to L2: This traversal ensures we compute dp[i][j] using already-known neighbor values as required by the minimum logic. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), traversing in wrong order (e.g., right to left might use uncomputed values), or not separating edge cases. Real code pattern uses loops with conditionals for bounds checking.

examples.trace: Start i=0: j=0,1,2. Then i=1: j=0,1,2. Then i=2: j=0,1,2. For (1,1), neighbors: top (0,1), left (1,0), top-left (0,0) – all visited earlier.

examples.output: For (0,0): no neighbors. For (0,1): no top or top-left. For (1,0): no left or top-left. For (1,1): neighbors (0,1),(1,0),(0,0).
examples.trace: At (0,0), set based on matrix. At (0,1), only left (0,0) exists? Actually for first row, only left might exist if j>0, but top and top-left don't. Similarly for first column.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum of Three Numbers
ladders.desc: Compute the smallest value among three given numbers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves taking three numbers as input and returning the minimum (smallest) value. The intuition is simple: in many algorithms, we need to determine the limiting factor or the worst-case scenario among multiple conditions. For example, in the maximal square problem, we use the minimum to ensure all adjacent squares are sufficiently large. The step-by-step method: Compare the first two numbers to find the smaller, then compare that result with the third number to find the overall minimum. Mathematically, min(a,b,c) = min(min(a,b), c). This can be done with conditional statements or built-in functions. Time complexity is O(1) since it involves a constant number of comparisons, and space complexity is O(1) as no extra data structures are needed. Connection to L3: This operation is applied during 2D traversal when accessing neighbor values to compute the DP recurrence. Common mistakes include incorrectly assuming the maximum or average, or not handling negative numbers (though in this context, values are non-negative). Real code pattern in Python: min(a, b, c) directly, or using if-else checks.

examples.trace: Compare 5 and 3: 3 is smaller. Compare 3 and 7: 3 is still smaller. So minimum is 3.
Compare 0 and 0: both are equal, minimum is 0. Compare 0 and 1: 0 is smaller. So minimum is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Matrix and Cell Values
ladders.desc: Understand a 2D grid where each cell is either 0 or 1.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces a binary matrix, which is a two-dimensional array (grid) where each element is either '0' or '1'. In the context of the maximal square problem, '1' represents a cell that can be part of a square, and '0' represents a cell that cannot. The intuition is that we are looking for contiguous regions of '1's that form a square shape. A matrix has rows and columns; for example, an m x n matrix has m rows and n columns. Cells are referenced by indices (i,j), where i is the row index (starting from 0) and j is the column index (starting from 0). Basic operations include accessing a cell's value, understanding its position, and recognizing that values are binary (only two states). This is prerequisite because the DP approach depends on checking if a cell is '1' to potentially extend a square. Step-by-step: Given a matrix, you can visualize it as a grid: row 0 at the top, column 0 at the left. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's about data representation. Connection to L4: The minimum operation in L4 uses values from such a matrix (via DP table, which derives from it). Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns, or misinterpreting '0' and '1' (e.g., thinking '1' means empty). Real code pattern: matrix[i][j] returns the value at that position.

examples.trace: Row 0: column 0 is '1', column 1 is '0'. Row 1: column 0 is '0', column 1 is '1'.
Only one row: indices 0,1,2 with values 1,1,0 respectively.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n) Space
approaches.summary: Optimize the DP approach by using only two rows or a 1D array to store previous DP values, reducing space usage while maintaining the same time complexity. Ideal for large matrices where memory is a constraint.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximal Square with O(n) Space DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding largest square of 1s in binary matrix using optimized DP with O(n) space.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the largest square containing only 1s in a binary matrix. The optimized DP approach reduces space from O(m*n) to O(n) while maintaining O(m*n) time. The intuition: for each cell (i,j), the size of the largest square ending at that cell depends on its three neighbors (left, top, top-left). We maintain only two rows of DP values: current row and previous row. For each cell, if matrix[i][j] = 1, then dp_curr[j] = min(dp_curr[j-1], dp_prev[j], dp_prev[j-1]) + 1, else dp_curr[j] = 0. We track the maximum value across all cells. The algorithm: 1) Initialize dp_prev as zeros of length n+1, max_side = 0. 2) For each row i: create dp_curr of zeros length n+1. For each col j: if matrix[i][j] = 1: dp_curr[j+1] = min(dp_curr[j], dp_prev[j+1], dp_prev[j]) + 1; max_side = max(max_side, dp_curr[j+1]). 3) Set dp_prev = dp_curr for next iteration. Return max_side * max_side. Time: O(m*n) visiting each cell once. Space: O(n) for two rows. Connection to L1: This uses the key recurrence relation with space optimization. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty matrix, off-by-one errors in indices, not resetting dp_curr each row. Real code pattern: def maximalSquare(matrix): if not matrix: return 0; m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]); dp_prev = [0]*(n+1); max_side = 0; for i in range(m): dp_curr = [0]*(n+1); for j in range(n): if matrix[i][j] == '1': dp_curr[j+1] = min(dp_curr[j], dp_prev[j+1], dp_prev[j]) + 1; max_side = max(max_side, dp_curr[j+1]); dp_prev = dp_curr; return max_side * max_side.

examples.trace: Initialize: dp_prev=[0,0,0], max_side=0. Row 0: dp_curr=[0,0,0]; j=0: '0' -> dp_curr[1]=0; j=1: '1' -> dp_curr[2]=min(0,0,0)+1=1, max_side=1; dp_prev=[0,0,1]. Row 1: dp_curr=[0,0,0]; j=0: '1' -> dp_curr[1]=min(0,0,0)+1=1; j=1: '0' -> dp_curr[2]=0; max_side remains 1. Result: 1*1=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence with Two Rows
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP recurrence using only previous and current rows to compute square sizes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the DP values for each cell using the recurrence relation with O(n) space. The intuition: to compute dp[i][j] (size of largest square ending at (i,j)), we need dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], and dp[i-1][j-1]. By storing only the previous row (dp_prev) and current row (dp_curr), we can compute dp_curr[j] using dp_curr[j-1] (left neighbor in current row), dp_prev[j] (top neighbor), and dp_prev[j-1] (top-left neighbor). The algorithm: For each row i: 1) Initialize dp_curr as zeros of length n+1 (with extra column for easier indexing). 2) For each column j: if matrix[i][j] = 1: dp_curr[j+1] = min(dp_curr[j], dp_prev[j+1], dp_prev[j]) + 1; else dp_curr[j+1] = 0. 3) Update dp_prev = dp_curr for next row. Time: O(n) per row, O(m*n) total. Space: O(n) for two rows. Connection to L0: This is the main loop in the full solution. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing leading to accessing wrong neighbors, not handling the first row/column specially. Real code pattern: def compute_dp_row(matrix_row, dp_prev, n): dp_curr = [0]*(n+1); for j in range(n): if matrix_row[j] == '1': dp_curr[j+1] = min(dp_curr[j], dp_prev[j+1], dp_prev[j]) + 1; return dp_curr.

examples.trace: Initialize dp_curr=[0,0,0,0]. j=0: matrix_row[0]='1' -> dp_curr[1]=min(dp_curr[0]=0, dp_prev[1]=1, dp_prev[0]=0)+1=1. j=1: '1' -> dp_curr[2]=min(dp_curr[1]=1, dp_prev[2]=0, dp_prev[1]=1)+1=1? Wait, min(1,0,1)=0, so +1=1. Correction: min(1,0,1)=0, dp_curr[2]=1. j=2: '0' -> dp_curr[3]=0. Result: [0,1,1,0].

Initialize dp_curr=[0,0,0,0]. j=0: '0' -> dp_curr[1]=0. j=1: '1' -> dp_curr[2]=min(dp_curr[1]=0, dp_prev[2]=1, dp_prev[1]=0)+1=1. j=2: '1' -> dp_curr[3]=min(dp_curr[2]=1, dp_prev[3]=2, dp_prev[2]=1)+1=2. Result: [0,0,1,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Min of Three Neighbors Recurrence
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 for matrix[i][j]=1.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how the size of a square ending at (i,j) depends on its three adjacent cells. The intuition: if matrix[i][j] = 1, then the largest square ending at (i,j) is limited by the smallest square among its top, left, and top-left neighbors. For example, if those neighbors can form squares of sizes 2, 3, and 2, then the maximum at (i,j) is min(2,3,2)+1 = 3. This works because all three directions must support the square. The algorithm: For each cell (i,j): if matrix[i][j] = 1: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1; else dp[i][j] = 0. Time: O(1) per cell. Space: O(1) for computation. Connection to L1: This recurrence is computed in the sub-routine. Common mistakes: forgetting the +1, applying to cells with value 0, misunderstanding the min operation. Real code pattern: if matrix[i][j] == '1': dp_val = min(top, left, top_left) + 1.

examples.trace: Since matrix cell is 1, compute min(2,3,2) = 2, then add 1: 2+1=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D DP Table for Square Problems
ladders.desc: Create and use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the side length of the largest square ending at (i,j).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up a DP table to store intermediate results for dynamic programming on grids. The intuition: for problems involving submatrices or subsquares, a DP table allows storing solutions to subproblems (e.g., largest square ending at each cell). We define dp[i][j] as the side length of the largest square of 1s that ends at cell (i,j) (i.e., has its bottom-right corner at (i,j)). The table is initialized with zeros, and we fill it row by row. The algorithm: 1) Create dp as a 2D list of zeros with dimensions m x n. 2) For i from 0 to m-1: for j from 0 to n-1: if matrix[i][j] = 1: if i=0 or j=0: dp[i][j] = 1 (base case); else: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1. 3) Track max value in dp. Time: O(m*n) to fill table. Space: O(m*n) for the table. Connection to L2: This table stores the values used in the recurrence. Common mistakes: incorrect base case handling, not initializing dp properly. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(m)]; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): if matrix[i][j] == '1': if i == 0 or j == 0: dp[i][j] = 1; else: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Grid Neighbors
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from adjacent cells in a 2D grid: top, left, and top-left neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing specific neighboring cells in a matrix or grid. The intuition: in grid-based DP, the value at a cell often depends on its neighbors. For a cell at position (i,j), the top neighbor is at (i-1,j), the left neighbor at (i,j-1), and the top-left neighbor at (i-1,j-1). We must handle edge cases where i=0 or j=0 (no top or left neighbor). The algorithm: To get neighbors for cell (i,j): top = value at (i-1,j) if i > 0 else some default (e.g., 0); left = value at (i,j-1) if j > 0 else default; top_left = value at (i-1,j-1) if i > 0 and j > 0 else default. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is used to fetch values from the DP table. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices, not checking bounds. Real code pattern: top = dp[i-1][j] if i > 0 else 0; left = dp[i][j-1] if j > 0 else 0; top_left = dp[i-1][j-1] if i > 0 and j > 0 else 0.

examples.trace: At (i,j)=(1,1): top is at (0,1): grid[0][1]=2; left at (1,0): grid[1][0]=4; top_left at (0,0): grid[0][0]=1.
At (0,1): i=0 so no top neighbor, use default 0; left at (0,0): grid[0][0]=10; i=0 so no top_left, use default 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Matrix Representation
ladders.desc: Understand and work with a 2D binary matrix where each cell is '0' or '1'.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves representing and manipulating a binary matrix. A binary matrix is a 2D grid where each element is either 0 or 1 (often represented as strings '0' and '1'). The intuition: such matrices model problems with two states (e.g., presence/absence). We access elements using row and column indices, starting from 0. For example, matrix[i][j] gives the value at row i, column j. The matrix has m rows and n columns. Basic operations: initialize a matrix, access a cell, iterate through rows and columns. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(m*n) to store. Connection to L4: This provides the input grid for neighbor access. Common mistakes: confusing row/column order, off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: matrix = [['1','0','1'], ['0','1','0']]; value = matrix[0][2] (gives '1').

examples.trace: Row index 1 is the second row: ['0','1']. Column index 0 is the first element: '0'.

examples.input: matrix = [['1','1','1'],['0','0','0']], iterate and print all values
